name: Mozarabic Way: Córdoba to Cerro Muriano
name_original: Cerro Muriano por el Camino Mozárabe desde Córdoba
description: This difficult 12.6-mile jogging route on the Mozarabic Way from Cordoba offers historical sites, natural landscapes, and challenging climbs
region_intro: Jogging around Campiña Baja offers a diverse range of routes through varied landscapes. The region features rolling hills, riverside paths, and areas with pine forests, providing a mix of terrain for runners. Many trails incorporate historical sights and offer views of the surrounding countryside. This area is well-suited for outdoor activities, with options ranging from gentle paths to more challenging routes with notable elevation gains. Best jogging routes around Campiña Baja The most popular jogging route is Guadalquivir Mills Trail, a 5.7 miles (9.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 4 minutes to complete. This route follows the Guadalquivir river, offering scenic views along its course. Another top favourite among local runners is Mozarabic Way: Córdoba to Cerro Muriano, a difficult 12.6 miles (20.3 km) path. This trail features significant elevation gain, leading through varied terrain between the two locations. Local runners also love the Guadalmellato Canal, Medina Azahara and San Jerónimo Loop, a 10.3 miles (16.6 km) trail leading through areas near the Guadalmellato Canal and historic sites, often completed in about 1 hour 50 minutes. Jogging around Campiña Baja is defined by riverside paths, rolling hills, and historic landscapes. The extensive network of routes offers options for different ability levels, from easy runs to more demanding trails. The routes in Campiña Baja are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 7000 runners have used komoot to explore Campiña Baja's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Campiña Baja?
A: Campiña Baja offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 400 options available. These routes cater to various preferences, from easy riverside paths to more challenging trails with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners in Campiña Baja?
A: Yes, Campiña Baja has numerous easy running routes perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed run. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001028080/race-along-the-guadalquivir-on-the-old-path-between-cordoba-and-seville" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Race along the Guadalquivir on the old path between Cordoba and Seville&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.1 km path that follows the river, offering a gentle and scenic experience.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see while jogging in Campiña Baja?
A: The region is characterized by diverse natural features, including rolling hills, riverside paths, and pine forests. You can also discover beautiful natural monuments like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/624215" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baños de Popea Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4700544" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;First Spring of the Bejarano Stream&lt;/a&gt;. Several routes also pass by picturesque lakes and reservoirs, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4572001" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navallana Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Campiña Baja?
A: Yes, many running routes in Campiña Baja are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000385986/lago-azul-circular-from-cordoba-to-cerro-del-aulagar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago Azul – circular from Córdoba to Cerro del Aulagar&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 6.9 km loop. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000351250/montilla-y-la-campina-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montilla y La Campiña – circular&lt;/a&gt;, which is a longer 12.6 km moderate route.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Campiña Baja?
A: The running routes in Campiña Baja are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.4 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 7,000 runners have explored the region, often praising the varied terrain, scenic riverside paths, and the blend of natural beauty with historical landscapes.

Q: Can I find running routes that pass by historical landmarks?
A: Absolutely. Campiña Baja's routes often weave through areas rich in history. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000419892/guadalmellato-canal-medina-azahara-and-san-jeronimo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalmellato Canal, Medina Azahara and San Jerónimo Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you near historic sites. You can also find routes that pass by significant landmarks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1275715" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Holy Martyrs&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/27642922" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mosque–Cathedral of Córdoba&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any challenging running trails for experienced runners?
A: Yes, Campiña Baja offers several challenging routes for experienced runners. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000484573/mozarabic-way-cordoba-to-cerro-muriano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mozarabic Way: Córdoba to Cerro Muriano&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 20.3 km path with significant elevation gain, providing a demanding experience through varied terrain. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001062582/bujalance-and-canete-de-las-torres-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bujalance and Cañete de las Torres – circular&lt;/a&gt;, a 17 km route.

Q: What is the best time of year for running in Campiña Baja?
A: The region is suitable for outdoor activities throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for running. Summers can be hot, especially in the middle of the day, so early morning or late evening runs are advisable. Winters are typically mild, making it possible to run year-round, though some trails might be muddier after rain.

Q: Are there running routes that follow rivers or canals?
A: Many routes in Campiña Baja feature picturesque riverside paths and follow canals. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000504773/guadalquivir-mills-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalquivir Mills Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a popular choice, following the Guadalquivir river and offering scenic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000419892/guadalmellato-canal-medina-azahara-and-san-jeronimo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalmellato Canal, Medina Azahara and San Jerónimo Loop&lt;/a&gt; also incorporates sections along the Guadalmellato Canal.

Q: What are the typical distances for running routes in Campiña Baja?
A: Running routes in Campiña Baja vary significantly in length. You can find shorter, easy runs around 4-7 km, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001028080/race-along-the-guadalquivir-on-the-old-path-between-cordoba-and-seville" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Race along the Guadalquivir&lt;/a&gt; (4.1 km). Moderate routes often range from 9-12 km, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000367016/tentecarreta-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tentecarreta Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt; (11.7 km). For longer distances, there are difficult trails exceeding 20 km, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000484573/mozarabic-way-cordoba-to-cerro-muriano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mozarabic Way: Córdoba to Cerro Muriano&lt;/a&gt; (20.3 km).

Q: Are there any viewpoints or scenic spots along the running trails?
A: Yes, many trails offer panoramic views of the surrounding countryside, especially those that involve some elevation gain. The region's rolling hills provide numerous vantage points. While specific viewpoints are not always named, the diverse landscapes, including pine forests and riverside areas, ensure a scenic backdrop for your run.
tips: This section of the Cañada Real also coincides with the Mozarabic Way and with some sections of the Córduba-Emérita Roman road, proof that it is an ancient path, something interesting to think about while you run here.
Tunnel with good pavement and in good condition, about 100 meters long. Carrying a flashlight is quite useful, because it makes a bit of a curve and halfway you can't see the exit of it.
This deposit is one of many in this area, where there was a lot of copper extraction. The complex has been declared an Asset of Cultural Interest due to its high historical value.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog this route?
faqs.answer: This route is approximately 20.3 kilometers (12.6 miles) long. For jogging, it typically takes around 2 hours and 30 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and fitness level. The route is classified as 'difficult' and requires very good fitness due to its elevation gain and varied terrain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse and challenging. You'll encounter mostly accessible paths, but also unpaved tracks (6.22 km), natural paths (1.96 km), and some asphalt sections (713 m). Be prepared for continuous uphill stretches, including a steep incline up Loma de los Escalones. Some areas, particularly where Roman road vestiges are carved out of limestone, require sure-footedness.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or viewpoints will I encounter?
faqs.answer: The route offers a rich blend of historical sites and natural beauty. You'll pass the Iglesia de Santiago and sections of Cordoba's 11th-12th century Muslim city wall. As you leave the city, you'll traverse Mediterranean scrub and oak forests. Highlights include a restored Roman bridge over the Pedroches stream, the shrine of Nuestra Señora de Linares with its medieval watchtower, and a small single-arch Roman bridge. From higher points, such as near Cerro Torreárboles, you can enjoy wonderful vistas of Cordoba and its rolling plains. You will also pass the Cañada Real Soriana, the Cerro Muriano Railway Tunnel, the Cerro Muriano Copper Mines, and the Piedra Horadada.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and demands 'very good fitness'. It features a total elevation gain of 450 meters and includes steep inclines and varied, sometimes technical, terrain. It is not recommended for beginner joggers.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this route, and how do I access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The route begins in the urban quarters of Cordoba. While specific parking information isn't provided, you would typically look for public parking options within Cordoba city center near the start of the Mozarabic Way. Public transport within Cordoba can also take you to the initial sections of the trail.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The route largely follows natural paths and ancient drover's roads, making it generally suitable for dogs. However, always ensure your dog is well-behaved, on a leash, and that you carry waste bags. Be mindful of livestock if present in rural sections and ensure your dog can handle the varied and sometimes challenging terrain.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog this route is typically during the cooler months of spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these periods, temperatures are more moderate, making the 20.3 km distance and 450m elevation gain more manageable. Summers in Cordoba can be extremely hot, and winters can be damp.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to jog this section of the Mozarabic Way. It follows public rights of way, including the ancient Cañada Real Soriana.

faqs.question: Are there any challenging sections or specific gear recommendations for this route?
faqs.answer: Yes, there are a few points to note. The route includes a railway tunnel, about 100 meters long, which has a good firm surface but a slight curve in the middle. A flashlight is highly recommended for navigating this section. Additionally, some parts of the Roman road vestiges are carved out of limestone and require sure-footedness. For cyclists, the walking route can be technically difficult, and the N432 road is advised for a section. Always wear appropriate jogging shoes with good grip and carry sufficient water, especially given the continuous uphill stretches.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer:
Yes, this route is a significant part of the larger

Camino Mozárabe

, which is an ancient pilgrimage route to Santiago de Compostela. It also overlaps with sections of the

Sendero de Sierra Morena

,

Escalones Cañada Real Soriana

,

Arrastraculos

, and

La bonita

. The trail is well-marked with Jacobean Way signposts (scallop and yellow arrow) and is also designated as GR-40.

faqs.question: Are there facilities like cafes or accommodation along the way?
faqs.answer: While the route starts in Cordoba with ample facilities, as you leave the city, services become less frequent. The destination, Cerro Muriano, does offer accommodations and services for those completing the stage. It's advisable to carry enough water and snacks for the duration of your jog, especially before reaching Cerro Muriano.
